caveats: a: BLS publishes the occupation mix at 488900, combining 488999 with packing and crating.
a: OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is a 2023 snapshot.
a: The range reflects judgment about task content; the source does not measure AI exposure or whether tasks are already automated.
rho: Survey adoption is not equivalent to labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: The Deloitte respondent mix is not reported at 488999 or lower-middle-market scale.
rho: Implementation will vary sharply between information-heavy coordinators and physical terminal or stockyard operations.
e: No public source reports the share of 488999 firms matching the recurring outsourced operator-led lens.
e: The provided firm count is already estimated and the code's residual nature makes activity classification unusually noisy.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not limited to the EBITDA band.
s5: Survey responses are intentions, allow multiple selections, and do not establish completed transactions.
s5: Industry-specific ownership age and transaction incidence are unavailable.
q: No public dataset identifies the contract mix or repricing behavior of 488999 firms.
q: Retention will differ materially across facility-use, per-transaction, fixed-fee, and labor-based arrangements.
d5: Employment is not constant-quality demand and may embed productivity changes.
d5: The projection covers 2024-2034 and NAICS 488900, not exactly the 2026-2031 horizon or 488999 lens.
d5: Different included activities can move in opposite directions.
o: The operator requirement is inferred from activity definitions and physical occupation mix rather than directly measured.
o: Coordination-only firms face much greater self-service and software-substitution risk than facility operators.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and institutional customers buy recurring, usage-based, per-transaction, or contracted support from a third-party operator. Revenue commonly follows service volume, facility use, labor, or a negotiated fixed service fee.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is narrowed to recurring outsourced operator-led services because the official examples span coordination businesses, stockyards, and asset-dominant pipeline terminals, whose operating and acquisition economics are too different for one coherent screen.
lens.excluded_activities: Independent pipeline terminal facilities and other businesses whose economics are predominantly ownership of specialized infrastructure rather than outsourced operating services; captive internal support units; one-off project work; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring outsourced transportation-support operations, including transport coordination and scheduling, terminal or stockyard support, and other repeat services where an accountable operator serves external customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and mean wages by occupation for the broader NAICS 488900, not task-level AI exposure for 488999. I wage-weight the mix conceptually: office, customer-service, billing, scheduling, sales-support, and management information tasks receive materially higher exposure than driving, handling, inspection, and facility work. Because 488900 also includes packing and crating, which is unusually physical, the broader mix likely biases exposure downward for the narrowed 488999 service lens, while assigning exposure from occupational titles can bias it upward where judgment and site presence dominate.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupation mix at 488900, combining 488999 with packing and crating.
OEWS excludes self-employed workers and is a 2023 snapshot.
The range reflects judgment about task content; the source does not measure AI exposure or whether tasks are already automated.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll-by-occupation and task-time studies from at least ten 488999 operators, split between coordination, terminal-support, and stockyard businesses, and map each task to current automation status and feasible AI substitution.
a.rationale: The broader industry employed 19.45% in office and administrative support, 6.85% in management, 4.74% in sales, and 2.95% in business and financial operations, but 50.30% in transportation and material moving. The opportunity is therefore concentrated in documentation, customer communications, billing, scheduling, exception triage, reporting, and managerial analysis rather than core physical operations.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in broader NAICS 488900 from 42,700 in 2024 to 44,400 in 2034, a 3.9% ten-year rise. Annualizing that change and applying five years yields approximately 1.02, used as a proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service demand. Employment can understate demand when productivity rises or overstate it when labor intensity increases, and 488900 includes packing and crating, so the range allows both directions.
caveats: Employment is not constant-quality demand and may embed productivity changes.
The projection covers 2024-2034 and NAICS 488900, not exactly the 2026-2031 horizon or 488999 lens.
Different included activities can move in opposite directions.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain 488999-specific real revenue and transaction-volume histories by activity from Census product-line data, terminal throughput records, customer procurement volumes, and operator same-location volumes, then build a five-year end-market forecast.
d5.rationale: Transportation-support demand should broadly follow freight, passenger movement, and use of specialized facilities. BLS expects modest rather than rapid employment growth in the broader industry, supporting a near-flat to gently growing five-year quantity outlook rather than a structural boom.

caveats: No public source reports the share of 488999 firms matching the recurring outsourced operator-led lens.
The provided firm count is already estimated and the code's residual nature makes activity classification unusually noisy.
e.change_evidence: Classify every 488999 firm in a commercial establishment database by primary activity, customer type, recurring revenue, infrastructure ownership, and owner dependence, then validate a stratified sample by management interview.
e.rationale: The official code includes outsourced transportation-support activities, but its illustrative examples span vanpool or carpool arrangement, independent pipeline terminals, and stockyards. A meaningful share should meet the recurring external-service lens, while asset-dominant terminals, captive operations, and businesses too dependent on a unique facility or owner fall outside the coherent operator-led target.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 488999 ($1,194,559,742) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 488999 ($1,114,355,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $277,410 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 488999); class <5: 535 firms, avg revenue/firm $740,789 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $72,820 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,707,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $266,172 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,269,903 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $223,131 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,575,235 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $842,946 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,826,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $867,620 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $20,000,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,966,000 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 9

caveats: The operator requirement is inferred from activity definitions and physical occupation mix rather than directly measured.
Coordination-only firms face much greater self-service and software-substitution risk than facility operators.
o.change_evidence: Interview procurement leaders at twenty customers and review churn records to quantify which services they expect to self-serve, replace with software, or continue outsourcing; test whether AI-native workflows can operate without a contracted accountable service provider.
o.rationale: Most included services coordinate or execute real-world transportation activity and carry responsibility for safe facility use, physical handling, customer exceptions, and service recovery. Software can remove calls, paperwork, and routine dispatch decisions, and some coordination may be self-served, but most year-five quantity should still require a locally accountable operator.

caveats: No public dataset identifies the contract mix or repricing behavior of 488999 firms.
Retention will differ materially across facility-use, per-transaction, fixed-fee, and labor-based arrangements.
q.change_evidence: Review a representative contract set and five years of renewals for pricing basis, cost disclosure, indexation, gain-sharing, service-level credits, rebid frequency, and price realization following documented productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee and contracted service periods permit an operator to retain much of an implemented efficiency initially, but usage-based or labor-linked pricing, customer procurement, contract renewal, and low-differentiation competition should share a substantial portion with customers over five years. Retention is higher where AI improves reliability and response time, and lower where savings are transparent or contracts are cost-plus.

rho.bridge: Deloitte surveyed more than 200 transportation executives, mostly at companies broader and larger than the target firms, and found over half had active but generally limited generative-AI implementations. Federal Reserve survey synthesis measures AI adoption across US firms, not implemented substitution of exposed labor. I translate those adoption signals downward into the share of exposed opportunity that a lower-middle-market 488999 operator can embed in production workflows within five years; larger-company readiness may bias the result upward, while rapid diffusion of inexpensive tools may make the current adoption snapshot bias it downward.
caveats: Survey adoption is not equivalent to labor substitution or avoided hiring.
The Deloitte respondent mix is not reported at 488999 or lower-middle-market scale.
Implementation will vary sharply between information-heavy coordinators and physical terminal or stockyard operations.
rho.change_evidence: Run twelve-month before-and-after pilots across billing, dispatch support, customer service, compliance documentation, and exception triage in three representative operators, recording accepted automated work, error rates, escalation rates, and avoided hires.
rho.rationale: Document and communication workflows are technically tractable, and current transportation adoption shows pilots are no longer unusual. Implementation remains constrained by fragmented operational systems, data quality, customer-specific exceptions, safety and liability review, and the need for accountable humans at facilities and during disruptions.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans among US employer-business owners: 22% planned to sell, transfer, or take public, versus the required realized probability of a qualifying control transfer among eligible 488999 lower-middle-market firms. I discount intentions for failed, delayed, partial, and nonqualifying transactions; the target firms' greater scale and operational substance may improve transferability relative to the survey's typical employer firm, partly offsetting that discount.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not limited to the EBITDA band.
Survey responses are intentions, allow multiple selections, and do not establish completed transactions.
Industry-specific ownership age and transaction incidence are unavailable.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed transaction data from business brokers, SBA 7(a) loan records, PitchBook, and state change-of-control filings for matched 488999 firms, including failed listings and family transfers.
s5.rationale: Employer businesses are substantially more succession-oriented than nonemployers, and 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. Still, plans do not reliably become completed control transfers, and specialized facilities, customer concentration, permits, or owner relationships can impede a sale.

principal_driver: A meaningful administrative and coordination layer sits above transportation work that still needs an accountable physical operator.
principal_weakness: The code is highly heterogeneous and the already-small estimated firm pool shrinks further after excluding asset-dominant and nonrecurring businesses.

run_meta.title: All Other Support Activities for Transportation

sources.supports: Defines 488999 and identifies its heterogeneous illustrative activities: vanpool or carpool arrangement, independent pipeline terminal facilities, and stockyards, while distinguishing excluded adjacent transportation services.
sources.title: North American Industry Classification System, United States, 2022

sources.supports: Reports 37,070 total jobs, including 19.45% office and administrative support, 50.30% transportation and material moving, 6.85% management, 4.74% sales, and 2.95% business and financial operations, with occupation-level wages.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 488900

sources.supports: Reports a July 2024 survey of more than 200 transportation executives: over half of surveyed companies had active but generally limited implementations, 71% expected transformation to take more than three years, and 40% cited data misuse as their top generative-AI risk.
sources.title: Gen AI shows early promise in the transportation industry

sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Projects employment in NAICS 488900 from 42,700 in 2024 to 44,400 in 2034, a 3.9% increase.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Synthesizes US surveys showing about 18% firm-level AI adoption at year-end 2025, 41% work-related generative-AI use among workers, and substantial differences by firm size and measurement method.
sources.title: Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy

title: All Other Support Activities for Transportation
